title: Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements
subtitle: Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.
caption:
Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements

Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.

The chart displays the cumulative number of parties for twelve distinct multilateral environmental agreements from 1971 to 2015. The Vienna Convention (Ozone) and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) show the earliest and most sustained growth, both starting near zero in the early 1970s and rising to approximately 190 to 200 parties by 2015. The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and the UN Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD) follow similar trajectories but begin their ascent slightly later, reaching between 180 and 195 parties by the end of the period. The Kyoto Protocol (Greenhouse gas emissions) and the World Heritage Convention exhibit steep increases starting in the late 1990s, plateauing near 190 and 185 parties respectively. The Cites Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora and the Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants reach final values between 160 and 175 parties. The Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety, Ramsar Convention (Wetlands), and UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) cluster in a lower band, ending between 150 and 165 parties. The Rotterdam Convention (Hazardous chemicals) and the Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals show the latest starts and lowest totals, finishing near 150 and 125 parties respectively. All lines demonstrate a monotonic increase over time, with no declines visible in any series across the 44-year span.
